text.title: I Found the WORLD'S MOST UNIQUE RESTAURANT
text.u: High up in the Andes, there are still
indigenous communities living in harmony
with nature in a way most of us have
long forgotten. And there is this man
together with his team who is carrying
this legacy forward. His name is Vir Leo
Martinez and he is one of the greatest
chefs of our time. He has built an
entire institution working side by side
with indigenous communities researching
and rediscovering forgotten ingredients.
And that work extends into restaurants
that might be one of the most unique in
the world. Really mind-blowing. But what
did the Incas know that we don't? During
our journey, we were given a glimpse
into this nearly forgotten world. We saw
how they live, what they can teach us,
and we step into the world that most of
us didn't even know still existed.
Welcome to Peru.
We are heading to the Sacred Valley, but
first we have to get to Cusco. To get
there, we need to fly southeast
direction from Lima. It's about a 90
minutes flight and it lies 3,400 m above
sea level.
>> It's already late in the night when we
are arrived, so we go to bed right away.
And in the morning, the culture lesson
begins. I am stoked.
I suspect it's called the Sacred Valley
for a reason. The atmosphere is
beautiful and charming. Fresh air,
soothing silence, breathtaking
landscape. I can't find the words. Then
our van arrives to take us to our first
stop, Urubamba.
This city is located on the southern
part of Peru in the Cusco region within
the sacred valley between the city of
Kusco and Machu Picchu at 2900 m high.
Roughly 20 to 25,000 people live here
now, but it was already an important
agricultural center back in the time of
the Inca Empire.
>> When we arrived, I realized pretty
quickly that it's going to be something
else.
>> Okay, be careful, man. Okay.
>> I have seen quite a lot of markets. I
have been to Chinese, French,
Portuguese, Arabic, Thai, Japanese
markets and so on. The one thing I found
common in them is they are constantly
buzzing.
But still, this one feels different.
Just look around. It's all rustic and
real. So we are actually in a very good
season because
actually the harvest of vegetable roots,
corn and some fruits actually coming
from the Amazon are here.
>> You can sense some kind of indescribable
energy and atmosphere.
>> Feel like happiness here.
>> Yeah.
>> I never feel it in a market. I have
visited many many markets and I really
love the French one. But now I'm I'm
feeling something.
>> Yeah. Smell. Smell. You see
>> beautiful.
>> Here in Peru, it's not easy to appear
with Vir Leo on your side. There is
someone at every corner who wants to get
a picture with him. He's kind of a
national treasure here. The hero of the
nation.
>> How much how much did you pay him?
>> Uh it's now 10 bucks.
>> Now, let's see what we can find here.
>> Okay, look at this giant corn. Well,
it's not just about the size. It's so
flavorful. Look at the shapes, you know.
It's no like perfect carrots, you know,
same sizes. No flour from from casava,
from quinoa, from different potatoes,
from corn. Look at these ones. Dry
potato,
all these kind of legumes.
>> You will find here the complete spectrum
of the rainbow in vegetables and herbs.
And there are many you might not have
seen or didn't know they were Peruvian.
>> Why? When we want to thicken the sauces,
we can use uh toasted quinoa. Uh
beautiful
>> maca, you know, like maca roots.
>> But that's that's a really happy one,
right?
>> And now it's quite trendy everywhere.
You know, if you go to fancy markets,
you can see lots of maca. You know, this
these bubbles, they grow, they are algae
and and they grow in the mountains, in
the lakes.
We reach one of the most iconic parts of
Peruvian cuisine, the potatoes.
Basically, Peru is the original homeland
of potato. There are more than 3 to
4,000 types here. Incredible numbers.
So, we can confidently say that Peru
takes the lead in this matter.
>> Experience.
>> This is the real rainbow, right?
>> I think you can find every color here.
We are going to get to know a lot about
them later. But now something happens I
didn't see coming. I don't understand a
single word in Spanish other than vamos.
But judging by the lady's smile and the
goats nearby, I suspect they are talking
about tasting the freshest ingredient on
the market.
Before I could ask for a bit of a break
to prepare, Leo is already in action.
Yeah, he's a man who acts. Then he says
the one word I know in Spanish.
>> Let's go.
>> Okay.
At first, I was a bit nervous because,
well, you know, I am being clumsy with
the goat that's in my hand. A kind lady
trying to tell me what to do. But let's
be honest, this situation is screaming
for a dirty joke. Meanwhile, I got
pretty good at it and started to pour
like a river. All right, more like a
creek. A very slow, very narrow creek.
After my attempts, the lady jumped in to
save the day and showed how it's done by
a real pro.
>> Okay, you are a bit better.
>> It's a profession after all.
Really great. And be
>> it's good, you know, the first sip in
the morning. Cheers.
>> This is really good. And with everything
around me here right now, I feel great.
I think it it was like um
>> 30 years ago when I last time I I drink
milk like that.
>> Really?
>> Yeah. Maybe 35 when I was um and it was
coming from a cow. So my grandma has
like four cows. And
>> but goat milk isn't only good for
drinking or making cheese. It is also a
great ingredient for desserts. And we
are about to try one.
>> A cup of ice cream made of goat milk
here by hand. I'm having an ice cream
next to the milk shop.
>> Delicious.
>> Now, this is what I call 0 kilometer
food. It's really good.
>> And the question is really, do you use
anything from here for your restaurants?
Normally the chef they come to this
market this called Urubama market and
they come at 5:00 in the morning to
source most of the plants and the
medicinal plants and then some some of
the new tubers that we're using on the
menu and then of course we have our our
ingredients up up in the mountain.
>> Yeah. Yeah.
>> This place is absolutely mind-blowing
but unfortunately we got here a bit too
late.
>> It's quite crowded. Uh, I mean now it's
a bit too late and it's around 7:00 in
the morning and it's late. Yeah. But you
know these people I mean these guys they
they they go to bed at 7 night 8 and at
4 4:30 5 they have everything already
set and the market opens.
Vision of life is different here in the
mountains.
>> Very different to what we see in Lima.
not a western uh you know thinking
>> definitely not
>> yeah this this is about the end of Peru
people here is just still counting the
money like this and you pay in cash and
and that's that's it no papers no
nothing no screens uh not at all this
mar has been working like this for well
100 years
let's go
>> to truly understand the roots of Aru, we
need to go back in time for a moment or
back to 1400 BC to the rise of the Inca
Empire when they were one of the biggest
and most advanced cultures of the world.
Their road system is one of the best
examples of their greatness. It
connected the empire over more than
40,000 kilometers through mountains,
deserts, and jungles. Their engineering
knowledge was ridiculously genius. To
this very day, there are still unsolved
mysteries about their structures, not to
mention their agriculture, which adapted
brilliantly to these lands. The Incas
not only produced food, but experimented
with the land. They observed the
microclimates, the temperature
differences, and the behavior of the
soil. One of the most remarkable
examples of this is Morai
and that's exactly where we are heading
at the moment.
So what you see here is like we have a
our our lab is there next to the
restaurant and then we have this
ancestral
pre Inca's lab. Morai is an
archaeological site within the sacred
valley and it is known for its unique
circular shape. In Ketwa they call it
Muyu. It has four muyus and the main and
most important one is this big one. So
what is it and how did it work? Let me
show you.
In this circular hole there are 12
terrases going downwards about 2 m
differences per teras. As we are moving
south, the climate is constantly
changing. At the bottom is warmer and
more humid. At the top, obviously colder
and drier. The lower levels simulated
the foggy woods around Cusco. The mid
levels the warm and dry climate of the
sacred valley. And the top ones
simulated the mountain environments of
the Andes.
This is where the Incas brought the
plants from the different regions and
they made them used to the new heights
and weathers. Then they observed where
they survived and stayed viable. Where
they bloomed, developed and brought
crops. Then they took the good seeds
from here and took it to the centers of
the empire where the mass production
could begin. Nowadays, this is not
working anymore. So we want to replicate
something like this in these different
elevations.
>> The energy that surrounds this place is
something incredibly special. Can't
describe it. This is something you got
to experience to understand. I am here
and I I I feel that I'm a little bit
speechless.
>> Yeah, I just love I just love to come
here like once a week. Uh this air and
the whole energy something different.
>> But enough about the wanding. We have
still got plenty to learn today.
Our next stop is the Mia Central, one of
the most fascinating restaurants on the
planet. By the end of this video, you
will understand everything.
It's a bit like being in school because
before lunch, we have to study a bit.
It's a research restaurant, so it's kind
of different from the ones we are used
to. As soon as we arrived, I sensed some
kind of pleasant sand, and it stayed
with us ever since. I love it.
Then we meet a kind young gentleman and
that look finally breaks.
>> I watch all his videos.
>> Please, please ask me to have a picture
because everybody's asking him.
Everybody's asking him.
>> Now you have now you have a fan. Yeah,
you have finally you found a fan.
>> What is the smell?
>> Palos Santo. That's a wood we burned. So
you have a nice aroma there.
Okay for you.
>> We meet our guide Mariel who is going to
be our guide in the following hours.
>> So today we're going to be going to the
community of Kahakai is one of the
communities that we work with.
>> I bet you have never heard that a modern
restaurant is working with indigenous
communities. Mia Centro is working
closely with the native Peruvian
communities. Told you this is not your
typical everyday restaurant. On one side
these people teaches generations of
knowledge about plants. seasons and
living along with the land. On the other
side, the researchers of meal extends
this knowledge with today's views and
interpretations.
This is how tradition and contemporary
philosophy shape the dishes together.
So, we are going to meet people from
these communities and see what a day
looks like in their lives.
>> His name is Cato and he's from the
Katarakai community. Sorry if I
misspelled it.
What he's about to show us is the
chicha, the ancestral fermented corn
drink of Peru.
>> Just like potatoes, corn has a lot of
types here.
>> Type of corn that we use to prepare
chicha deora is the small one that is
pukuto.
This is small size corn that grows up
these mountains and that concentrates
more sugar. And to concentrate more
sugar of this corn, we're going to
germinate it.
Chicha is the base for all the work in
the field. Without chicha, we cannot
work in the field.
>> Before drinking, they always offer a few
drops to mother earth and the mountain
spirits or in the ketua language, patcha
mama and the apples. It's a kind of
ritual mutuality. We give before we got.
It tastes slightly sweet and mildly sour
because of the fermentation. Mama.
>> So then today we're going to be learning
about the medicine as well in the Andes.
This is olo.
>> It's a fresh plant and for example, if
you have a toothachake, you take a slice
of it, put it on your cheek and it
relieves the inflammation.
>> This purple one is a tuber.
>> There are different varieties of this
one. The dark one, it's medicinal. It's
really spicy.
>> Nice interesting texture. Really fresh
and also very juicy.
>> Yes. It's it's it's like horseradish.
Beautiful heat. So whenever you cook
this one, it's really really sweet.
>> It quinoa.
>> Kinoa originally comes from the aliplano
within Bolivia and Peru. Whenever we
were kids, our mom will be cooking
different textures of kinoa. And that's
why maybe probably the nowadays we don't
get sick easily.
>> You know the people here have guinea
pigs, apacas, and sheep. A lot of meat
if you think about it. Yet they don't
eat meat that much. Meat here is mostly
for special occasions. Still, you're
going to get some protein from time to
time. This is when this little superfood
enters the picture.
>> So then tari, it's family of loopins.
All right, so it's really bitter. This
tari contains more than 17% of protein.
So it's a super food as well. Okay,
>> I won't say I feel like a true
researcher, but I got to say this is
really exciting so far and I enjoy
myself very much.
We say a big thank you to GLTO and move
on to the next stop where we meet a new
face.
>> Let me introduce to John Czecha. It's
the aronomist and he is the leader of
the Chakra project.
>> As Vio mentioned before, they are
replicating the work of Mor as well.
>> In Peru, we have more than 3,000
varieties of potatoes. And in the
Chakra, we have around 180 varieties of
potatoes. So the idea of this project is
that we can get different varieties from
different parts or regions and then we
can grow at this level and then big gift
to the communities so they can provide
to the restaurants.
>> And here we can see some of the potatoes
they grow right here. Different sizes,
shapes and colors. We already saw at the
market how many varieties exist here. So
actually the pigments of the potatoes
depends on the altitude where it grows
and the soil also is very important for
the pigments. So if we grow these
potatoes at these mountains, the colors
will be more intense than this. But if
we grow that one in the sacred valley,
they will lose the antioxidant and the
pigments.
>> I have tried several types around the
world, but these are different,
especially compared to the ones at home
in Europe. This is beautiful. Look at
the color. It's a little bit juicier
than the normal potato.
>> Yes. Do you think you can peel this one?
>> Actually, this potato is a test in the
communities.
>> Really?
>> It is. If the woman wants to get
married, she must peel the potato. If
she cannot peel,
>> so she's done. She cannot get married.
And the skin manos must not break.
>> So with one go.
>> Yes.
>> No. No.
>> You already break. That's why they call
kachuachi. It means uh making cry to the
daughter-in-law.
>> Meanwhile, we have bumped into Viru and
I proudly tell him what I have learned.
>> And I also gave him a protein.
>> If you want to have a great recipe, this
is just an idea for you.
>> You know me, I have where I can.
>> Our next guide will be Florinda. This
wonderful lady walks here every day for
about 40 45 minutes. And today she will
introduce some special plants to us.
>> This thing on the side of the road is
called cha.
>> Cherche is a ketcho word and means
flirty because the woman in the Andes
they were used to paint their lips. It
has a lot of pigments and antioxidants.
Is the fruit of the Andes because around
here we don't have bananas, peaches,
oranges. So the kids when they go to the
field they will start eating wild
fruits. The bark also it's medicine. So
we eat a lot of cha the berries. So
we're going to start having to egg. So
we can have the bark of these and start
chewing. So it will work like
anesthesia.
>> Everywhere I step I see something wide
or at least wide by their look. But I
already know we are going to stop there
and learn that is used for something.
>> So the heat in the Andes is really
strong. So the women's prevent by this
way.
>> Okay, I need to try this. I can't
believe you. Let's try some worm plants.
Sounds good, right?
>> Marco is a very strong aromatic plant
full of essential oils.
>> H Marco we're going to use to preserve
our potatoes.
>> Once we harvest the potatoes, we cover
with this plants such as muna and marco
and they will preserve longer. Also
these aromatic herbs will keep away the
bags and insects from the potatoes.
>> How clever. Yet
>> to me it's simple amazing how versatile
one single plant can be and I grew up on
a farm so let's say I am more
experienced in these things than most
but this here is something totally
different and if we are already
mentioned muna let's taste it too
>> muna is considered the Indian mint
because of the minty flavor but it's
family of oregano really good for
digestion stomach aches altitudes
>> it's mint it's basil it's everything
>> yes
>> beautiful flavors
They use it in soups and as I mentioned
before preserving potatoes
>> and actually some mummies in
archaeologic sites were found that stuck
it with mommy uh with muna leaves.
>> These worm plants are useful but you
can't consume much of them. You will get
inflammation.
This one then this citrus looking fruit
is called tumbo. It is actually a banana
passion fruit and it is native in the
Andes in Peru. It contains a lot of
vitamin C, fiber, and is digestive.
>> Around here, it's used for uchuka and
Peru's iconic dish, savvicha. So, it's
really acidic and in a way you can sense
the metal.
>> We are getting close to me, our final
destination for today. Actually, we are
already here. But first, one little
detour.
This is a place where some of the finest
yarns are made by hand.
Das
>> they are Bhinia and Modesta. They are
coming from the community of also they
come like 1 hour walking away and on
their way they will be collecting plants
and botanics to dye during the day.
>> Now there are only two ladies here but
in total there are 16 who used to work
here. If you know something about
Peruvian textiles then you know they use
apaka and sheepool for making yarns.
She's doing the first trip out of the
shipwool. So here they work with sheep
wool only, not with alpaka. Alpacas are
more than 4,000 mters above sea level.
So yeah, it's hard to take care about
the alpacas. I am fascinated the way
they make threads, how their hands move.
It seems so easy. But behind those
delicate movements, there is a lifelong
knowledge. But how does it connect to
meal? Well, it's simple. It's about
preserving a tradition. You know in
today's world we used to machineate
clothes. This here is a thing that being
extinct and this way they can preserve
this tradition. But as this kind lady
points out,
>> but you know the thing that has more
value is the things made by hand.
>> Couldn't agree more.
And now our final destination
meal.
This place is amazing. Everything we saw
outside concentrates here in this one
building.
>> We start at the bar. Let's see those
herbs in action. The first one is a wine
from Kusco infused with something we
know very well by now. Marco. The wine
itself is nice, fresh, acidic, but the
flowers make it more, how should I say
it, stronger, and also sweeter.
>> The next we try is an infusion of
Peruvian root. Some tichamali and some
reduction of cornhusk and kabuya. You
can guess only by the ingredients that
it has an earthy aroma. And because of
this earthy notes, my brain is connected
to beetroot. On blind, I would say
beetroot. but it's also mighty sweet.
It's a nice drink and it's actually
alcoholic.
The third one is even more earthy than
the previous one. It has been refreshed
with some herbs and it is balanced out
with some cacao
on the nose. That's the most beautiful.
>> Oh my god. It is meant to clean our
pallets and it is very refreshing
indeed. It does the job perfectly.
Thank you.
>> Then we move onto the dining area.
>> The design perfectly resembles the theme
and the landscape. It's rustic, pure,
but also set up with great care.
>> There are different scents and aromas
floating in the air. Fresh, interesting,
and very pleasant. I enjoy this very
much. It is so peaceful and different,
similar to what I felt in Central.
We will have a lunch menu here with six
savory courses and two sweets.
We are served our first drink which is
made of tumbo and kabuya.
>> This is my title drink.
>> It's beautiful.
Well, this is definitely special. Just
look at that. Such a simple thing but so
many details organized with great care
and it can be told about literally
everything else on the table. The level
of the details in everything and the
craftsmanship is just
really really mind-blowing.
So our first bites arrive.
>> Here's our first preservation area.
>> First of all, I love this gentleman very
much. Oh,
>> he sets the wine.
>> Enjoy. Thank you very much.
>> We have a couple of things on the table.
First, potato stems. Then we have
choplas. It is traditional dough made
with cocoa leaf flour stuffed with oka
and tuber. With them, we have elderberry
butter, which sounds really exciting. We
also have some potato chips. Then last
but not least, ukuta, a traditional
chili sauce. It's made with wild tree
potatoes and spread on potato dough.
The potato stem is very earthy, salty,
and a natural light sweetness, too.
Beautiful.
You know how I feel about bread and
butter. Even when it's so different from
what I'm used to, this is still
unbeatable. Oh my god. The chili sauce
is also very exciting, especially
because I feel the herb notes here and
there. Super interesting because we
already tried those. It's pleasantly
spicy and has a sharp acidity. Very,
very nice.
I am simply amazed by how much work goes
into these beautiful utensils. I know I
talk about them a lot, but honestly,
just look at that.
Our next drink is made of kur and karob
tree. With this, we are going to the
highlands. The main attraction is lamb,
but we have a couple of things on the
side. Cocoa sour cream, kuchuro, which
is an algae from the highland altitudes.
Cherimoya or custard apple on the common
tongue and kiva, which is a type of
kinoa. For this moment, we suggest to
taste them individually and highly
recommend to mix them all in the vessel
in front of you.
>> I had the smell of this white flour.
It's like jasmine note. Beautiful. I
love how everything we tried on the road
comes back here in some form. Most of
the flowers, scents, and flavors are
clearly recognizable.
All of the elements are already good on
their own. So, we have the meaty, we
have the florally, we have the acidity,
and we have this crunchy element here.
>> Now, let's see how they work together.
Yeah, as I expected, it's wonderful. You
feel that maybe this is the freshest
bite you have ever had in a restaurant.
>> Next up is a 24 up red wine. It's from
2,800 m elevation. The aroma is nice.
It's very fruity.
>> They say there is a first to everything.
Well, I am about to taste apocalpa
cooked under slow fire for over 14
hours.
Alpaca rib farm lettuce and we have
tomato textures on a palos santo egg
yolk charcoal.
>> Okay, I am officially blown away. I have
been here before, but it has changed a
lot. When Vio asks what do I feel?
>> What do you feel?
>> I I feel um huge progress since my last
visit.
>> Happy to hear that.
>> Huge progress. The alpaka meat is weird
in a good way. Can't describe the flavor
properly, but it's slightly smoky, a bit
chewy, and there is a pleasant sweetness
in it.
This thing with flowers on top, it seems
like a light dish. Under the florals,
there are beautiful rich flavors.
>> It's an alpaka stew, strong, dark, rich,
but it's balanced out with these fresh
petals. Technically, you have a stew
which is hot and then you made a salad
with some smokeoky elements and
sweetness, crunchiness
and you mix together two different
temperature, two different flavor
profile and I haven't eaten anything
like this. They also prepare a wet towel
for this since you are supposed to use
your hands. And again, another familiar
scent hits my nose.
>> When we smell this uh
>> demon, the mint of the Andes, which we
tried before,
culture and gastronomy combined together
and elevated to perfection.
>> Our next drink is on the way with Vio on
its side.
>> So, you know what we what he he does? He
toast the the husk of the corn as you
can see here. And then you put very cold
water and then you infuse for one night.
That's the result.
>> Crazy.
>> It's like a this kind of explosion and
so intense but in the end very light.
We'll see.
>> We continue the meal with another
Peruvian icon corn.
>> So we're using 100% of this corn. We are
using four varieties of corn from our
harvest uh last month harvest. And you
will find all these different colors,
textures. And uh we use the skin, we use
the very meaty parts. Then we use the
the husk and we use the inside part of
the corn. And uh then you make a butter
out of the creamy part like a the juice
like the milk coming out of the corn.
But it's fresh, you know. We squeeze the
corn fresh and we get all this juicy
milky corn. Enjoy.
It's official. He is crazy.
>> Wonderful flavors. I don't know if they
use anything else for this, but it's
clearly about the corn. It is very
characteristic.
>> The next drink is Oxalis.
>> That's the first drink I don't really
like. It's acidic, a little bit bitter.
It doesn't mean it's not good. is just
not for me. With the next dish, we are
going to celebrate the harvest of
potatoes. I love the presentation here
and if I understand it correctly, they
use the same soil to cook them which
they have grown in. Then we have some
dips. So, it's like an ancient party
food. We also have charred pumpkin
cooked the same way with some herbs. It
is as simple as it sounds and it doesn't
even have to be more. pure strong
flavors. I am a huge fan of potatoes
anyway, so with a bit of charcoal and
salt, it's perfect. Not to mention the
dips on the side, which are crazy good.
A meat course arrives. This will be the
duck, I suspect. On one plate, there is
mushroom covered by Siciliano. It's a
vegetable from the belly. On the other
side is a salad made from avocado and
crispy tarvi. In the middle there are
three pieces of dough. All made from the
same tarby and covered with dried duck
meat.
>> The recommendation here is to start with
the black rock with the spoon.
>> Huge umami flavors.
>> Then the dough with your hands and eat
it in one or two bites.
>> The dry duck is very intense, strong. I
love it.
>> It's really different what I was
expecting.
>> So far, I don't even know how to
describe the experience. I would usually
use the good old beautiful, but this is
way more than that.
>> You see, it's something unique. But
since I was making this coloring process
just like 3 hours ago, you know, it's
just
we arrived at the dessert part.
>> I just read coil muna two barashes and I
have no idea. I literally have no idea
what it's going to be.
>> The first one is called frozen
cordillera. In front of you we have the
jakom. It is a sweet root in different
textures. Across the table we have
pushkai muna and pampis ice.
>> Wonderful dessert. Everything in it
tells you about the land. It's really
different than most of the dessert I
have ever tried in my life.
>> The next and last one is a showcase of
cacao textures. We have a spaghetti like
variation and an infusion. A crumble
that resembles the nibs and a
circle-like meringue with a foamy mousse
beneath it.
Beautiful expressions.
And with that, we reach the end of this
very special journey. It's time to say
goodbye and sum up how I feel right now.
These days, simply cooking good food for
the guest sometimes is just not enough.
That's why so many restaurants try to
tell stories about themselves. Louder
stories, bigger stories, more extreme,
more spectacular, more outrageous. What
we saw here today is also a story. The
crucial difference is that this one is
real. There is a huge amount of work
behind it. And let's be honest, this is
not the easiest path to run a
restaurant. For them, this not just
branding, it's a mission. Here the story
is reality. When you come here, you
don't just experience a restaurant. You
experience history, culture, and legacy.
All of it happening right there in the
place where it truly belongs. The people
working here alongside Vir Leo have
decided their lives to making this
visible to the world.
